title: Road safety crisis scale
text: WHO road safety material reports that road traffic crashes kill about 1.19 million people each year and are a leading cause of death for children and young adults. More than 90 percent of road traffic deaths occur in low- and middle-income countries, so BIMSTEC road users need locally understandable safety and enforcement information.
source_title: WHO Road traffic injuries fact sheet

title: Speed and crash severity
text: Speed management is central to road safety. Higher average speed increases both the probability of a crash and the severity of crash outcomes, especially where pedestrians, cyclists, and two-wheeler riders share the road with larger vehicles.
source_title: WHO Road traffic injuries fact sheet

title: Helmets and seat belts
text: WHO guidance treats certified helmets, seat belts, child restraints, sober driving, and speed control as core interventions. RoadLegal should pair legal penalty information with safety coaching because penalties are most useful when users understand the injury risk behind the rule.
source_title: WHO Road traffic injuries fact sheet

title: India speed limits and penalties
text: The Motor Vehicles Act framework allows government authorities to set maximum speed limits and penalises driving above the lawful limit. For RoadLegal, overspeeding should be answered by combining the national Act, the local speed notification, road type, vehicle class, and the local compounding schedule.
source_title: India Motor Vehicles Act, 1988

title: India helmet rule
text: India's Motor Vehicles Act includes protective headgear requirements for two-wheeler riders, with exemptions and details handled by the Act and rules. In the challan calculator, helmet violations should be limited to two-wheeler riders and passengers unless a local rule says otherwise.
source_title: India Motor Vehicles Act, 1988

title: India drink driving
text: India's Motor Vehicles Act penalises driving or attempting to drive while under the influence of alcohol or drugs. The RoadLegal answer should warn that this can involve a fine, possible imprisonment, court process, and stronger consequences for repeat offences.
source_title: India Motor Vehicles Act, 1988

title: India driver documents
text: Traffic enforcement commonly requires drivers to produce licence, registration, insurance, and pollution-control documents when lawfully requested. RoadLegal should ask for vehicle class and jurisdiction before calculating document-related challans.
source_title: India Motor Vehicles Act, 1988

title: Bangladesh Road Transport Act starter note
text: Bangladesh road-law answers should be grounded in the Road Transport Act, 2018 and current BRTA or government schedules. Until the latest fine schedule is reviewed, RoadLegal should explain the legal issue, cite the source, and avoid inventing an amount.
source_title: Bangladesh Road Transport Act, 2018

title: Nepal motor vehicle act starter note
text: Nepal road-law answers should use the Motor Vehicles and Transport Management Act, 2049 together with current traffic police fine schedules. For fines, RoadLegal should ask about vehicle type, location, and offence facts before giving a legal-review-needed estimate.
source_title: Nepal Motor Vehicles and Transport Management Act, 2049

title: Bhutan RSTA starter note
text: Bhutan road-law answers should use the Road Safety and Transport Act and current Road Safety and Transport Authority rules. RoadLegal can provide safety coaching offline, but challan amounts should remain marked for review until the latest RSTA schedule is ingested.
source_title: Bhutan Road Safety and Transport Act, 1999

title: Sri Lanka motor traffic starter note
text: Sri Lanka road-law answers should use the Motor Traffic Act and current police or court penalty schedules. RoadLegal should distinguish between spot fines, court-referred offences, and offences that can affect licence status.
source_title: Sri Lanka Motor Traffic Act reference

title: Thailand land traffic starter note
text: Thailand road-law answers should be grounded in the Land Traffic Act and current Royal Thai Police or regulatory fine schedules. Cross-border guidance should highlight left-hand traffic, local document requirements, and speed-limit signs.
source_title: Thailand Land Traffic Act reference translation

title: Thailand left-side driving and road position
text: Thailand traffic operates on the left side of the road. RoadLegal should warn cross-border travellers to adjust lane position, roundabout habits, overtaking expectations, pedestrian scanning, and mirror checks before driving in Thailand.
source_title: Thailand Land Traffic Act reference translation

title: Thailand speed signs and road class
text: For Thailand speed questions, RoadLegal should ask for road class, posted sign, vehicle type, and whether the user is inside a built-up area. Posted speed-limit signs and current police notices should control the final advice and fine estimate.
source_title: Thailand Land Traffic Act reference translation

title: Thailand motorcycle helmet safety rule
text: Thailand motorcycle guidance should tell both riders and passengers to wear a fastened protective helmet. For the calculator, helmet violations apply mainly to motorcycles and similar two-wheelers, and the final fine should be verified from the current Royal Thai Police schedule.
source_title: Thailand Land Traffic Act reference translation

title: Thailand seat-belt safety rule
text: Thailand car and van guidance should encourage every occupant to use an available seat belt. RoadLegal should avoid quoting a final seat-belt fine until the current Land Traffic Act amendment and Royal Thai Police comparison-fine schedule are reviewed.
source_title: Thailand Land Traffic Act reference translation

title: Thailand drink-driving enforcement
text: Thailand drink-driving guidance should be strict: do not drive after alcohol or drugs. Enforcement can involve roadside testing, court referral, licence consequences, fine, imprisonment, or stronger outcomes if a crash or repeat offence is involved.
source_title: Thailand Land Traffic Act reference translation

title: Thailand traveller driving documents
text: For tourists and cross-border travellers in Thailand, RoadLegal should prompt users to carry a valid driving licence accepted in Thailand, passport or ID evidence, vehicle registration or rental documents, insurance evidence, and emergency contact numbers.
source_title: Thailand Department of Land Transport

title: Thailand enforcement and help directory
text: Thailand local directory content should include Royal Thai Police for traffic enforcement, Highway Police for highway incidents, Department of Land Transport for licences and vehicle registration, Tourist Police 1155 for visitors, and emergency 191 or 1669 depending on the situation.
source_title: RoadLegal Thailand directory starter

title: Thailand scenario game: night market scooter
text: Scenario: a visitor rents a scooter near a night market and the passenger says a helmet is unnecessary for a short trip. Safe choice: stop until both rider and passenger have fastened helmets, check lights, avoid alcohol, and keep speed low around pedestrians.
source_title: RoadLegal Thailand game content

title: Thailand scenario game: monsoon highway
text: Scenario: rain starts on a Thai highway and the posted limit is higher than the safe speed for visibility. Safe choice: slow below the posted maximum, increase following distance, avoid sudden braking, and stop safely if visibility collapses.
source_title: RoadLegal Thailand game content

title: Thailand overspeeding maximum penalty from September 2022
text: Thailand's Government Public Relations Department reported that the traffic-law changes effective 5 September 2022 set a maximum fine of THB 4,000 for overspeeding, red-light violations, and failure to stop at a pedestrian crossing. Posted signs, road class, vehicle type, and the facts of the offence still control the applicable speed and issued penalty.
source_title: Thailand PRD traffic penalties effective September 2022

title: Thailand helmet and seat-belt maximum penalties from September 2022
text: Thailand's Government Public Relations Department reported that the traffic-law changes effective 5 September 2022 set a maximum fine of THB 2,000 for wrong-way driving, failure to wear a motorcycle helmet, and failure to wear a seat belt. Helmet requirements apply to motorcycle riders and passengers; seat-belt application can depend on the covered seating position and vehicle configuration.
source_title: Thailand PRD traffic penalties effective September 2022

title: Thailand drink-driving penalties from September 2022
text: Thailand's Government Public Relations Department reported that a first drink-driving offence under the changes effective 5 September 2022 carries a fine of THB 5,000 to 20,000 and imprisonment up to one year. A repeat drink-driving offence within two years carries a fine of THB 50,000 to 100,000 and imprisonment up to two years. BAC, driver category, crash involvement, injury, refusal, and later amendments can change the charge or court outcome.
source_title: Thailand PRD traffic penalties effective September 2022

title: Thailand driver points and licence suspension
text: Thailand's Government Public Relations Department reported that drivers begin with 12 points. Drink driving or road racing deducts four points; wrong-way driving or running a red light deducts two points. Reaching zero points can lead to a 90-day suspension covering all vehicle categories. Driving while suspended can lead to imprisonment up to three months, a fine up to THB 10,000, or both. Drivers with fewer than six points may attend approved training to recover points, subject to current rules.
source_title: Thailand PRD driver licence points enforcement

title: Myanmar vehicle law starter note
text: Myanmar road-law answers need current official source review before final fine amounts are displayed. The app should still provide cautious safety advice, document checklists, and a source-needed status for penalty calculations.
source_title: Myanmar Vehicle Law reference

title: Cross-border law interpreter
text: For cross-border BIMSTEC travel, RoadLegal should compare the previous and current jurisdictions, warn when fine data is unverified, and produce a pre-travel checklist: licence validity, registration, insurance, helmet/seat-belt requirements, local emergency contacts, speed signs, side-of-road rules, and e-challan payment portals.
source_title: RoadLegal architecture note

title: Offline RAG design
text: The offline RoadLegal design stores legal passages, structured fine records, and source metadata locally. It first retrieves relevant passages, then answers with citations. If a small local model is available, it can generate a fluent answer; otherwise the app provides a deterministic extractive answer from retrieved text.
source_title: RoadLegal architecture note
